title: Quantitative measurement of fluid inertial effects in confined Brownian motion
authors: Quentin Ferreira, Pablo Palacios-Alonso, Harshit Joshi, Rafael Delgado-Buscalioni, Yacine Amarouchene, Thomas Salez
abstract: The hydrodynamic response of Brownian particles in liquids is fundamentally altered by inertial forces arising from unsteady momentum transport in the surrounding fluid. These forces are of two distinct types\,: the added mass and the history effect. While both are well understood in bulk and weakly-confined geometries, under deterministic driving, their respective behaviours under strong confinement and thermal fluctuations remain scarcely addressed, unclear and often entangled together. The goal of the present study is thus to fill this fundamental gap. The behaviours of the two distinct inertial contributions are quantitatively investigated in the vicinity of a flat, rigid wall, using a combination of broadrange thermal colloidal-probe atomic-force-microscopy experiments, advanced numerical simulations and theory. The separation of the added-mass and history-force contributions is achieved through their different frequency-scaling signatures within the measured high-resolution thermal spectra. Our results establish a complete picture of Brownian motion at interfaces, in the lubrication regime, with direct relevance to nanofluidics and interfacial biophysics.
summary: This work quantitatively investigates how inertial forces—specifically added mass and history effects—alter Brownian motion when particles are strongly confined near a solid wall. By combining high-resolution AFM experiments with advanced simulations, the authors successfully separate these distinct hydrodynamic contributions across various confinement regimes. The results provide crucial insights for nanofluidics and interfacial biophysics by mapping out the full frequency-dependent drag response.
main_problem: To quantitatively investigate the hydrodynamic response of Brownian particles near a rigid wall, specifically separating and understanding the distinct contributions of fluid inertial effects (added mass and history effect) under strong confinement and thermal fluctuations.
main_result: The study successfully establishes a complete picture of Brownian motion at interfaces by quantifying how added-mass and history-force contributions change with confinement, confirming theoretical models like Brenner's result for the steady-viscous drag.
method: A combination of broadrange thermal colloidal-probe Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) experiments and advanced numerical simulations using spectral fluid solvers was employed.
model_or_system: The system involves a colloidal probe attached to an AFM cantilever moving in a Newtonian fluid near a planar rigid substrate. The analysis focuses on the complex friction coefficient, $\gamma$, which captures the full hydrodynamic response.
key_observables: Positional power spectral density (PSD), and the fitted dimensionless coefficients quantifying contributions: $c_0$ (steady-viscous), $c_B$ (history-force), and $c_m$ (added-mass).
important_parameters:
Confinement ratio ($D/R$), frequency ($	ext{Wo} \propto f/f_
u$), and the separation distance $D$. The study spans from bulk to deep lubrication regimes.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the decomposition of the friction coefficient $\gamma$ into its three distinct contributions ($c_0, c_B, c_m$) remains valid even in the presence of strong wall confinement.
figures_summary: Figures show the influence of the wall on the thermal spectrum (PSD) across various separations $D$, and plot the fitted parameters ($c_0, c_B, c_m$) as functions of $D/R$ comparing experiment to theory.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the hydrodynamic response by first establishing the experimental setup and diagnostics using PSDs. It then tackles each inertial contribution (steady-viscous, history force, added mass) separately, validating theoretical predictions against both experimental measurements and numerical simulations across varying confinement regimes.
why_interesting: While focused on classical fluid dynamics, the quantitative separation of memory effects (history force) and inertia (added mass) in confined systems provides a rigorous framework for analyzing non-equilibrium fluctuations relevant to open quantum systems or particle transport near boundaries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In addition, the respective fits to Eq. (2) (solid lines) are shown.
